preamble: The shift from the voiced palatal stop [ɟ] to the voiced postalveolar affricate [dʒ] (j) is a common form of affrication, often representing the final stage of palatalization. The instability of the large contact area of the palatal stop frequently leads to a turbulent, fricative release.

examples.note: Historical palatalization and affrication of Latin /ɡ/ before front vowels.
Palatalization of Germanic geminates into palatal stops and finally postalveolar affricates.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
